title: KDIGO 2025 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Immunoglobulin A Nephropathy (IgAN) and Immunoglobulin A Vasculitis (IgAV)
summary:
This document provides a supplement to the KDIGO 2025 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Immunoglobulin A Nephropathy (IgAN) and Immunoglobulin A Vasculitis (IgAV). It includes tables, figures, and supplementary material related to the guideline, such as factors for treatment selection, patient characteristics, treatment targets, and results from clinical trials. The guideline focuses on developing and implementing evidence-based recommendations for managing IgAN and IgAV.
This document details the methodology used for developing the KDIGO 2025 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Immunoglobulin A Nephropathy (IgAN) and Immunoglobulin A Vasculitis (IgAV). It outlines the work group membership, abstract, summary of recommendation statements, and systematic review topics used in the guideline development process.
This document outlines the specific management guidelines for IgAN and IgAV based on clinical evidence. It provides recommendations for screening, diagnosis, and treatment, including dietary modifications, medication management, and specific drug therapies. The guidelines aim to offer evidence-based approaches for clinicians managing these conditions.
This document provides a summary of the key recommendations and practice points from the KDIGO 2025 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Immunoglobulin A Nephropathy (IgAN) and Immunoglobulin A Vasculitis (IgAV). It focuses on the specific treatments and management strategies outlined in the guideline for these conditions.
This document outlines the specific dietary recommendations for patients with IgAN and IgAV. It focuses on limiting sodium, potassium, and phosphorus intake, providing specific food guidelines and meal planning strategies based on different stages of kidney disease.
This document provides a summary of the specific pharmacologic therapies evaluated in the KDIGO 2025 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Immunoglobulin A Nephropathy (IgAN) and Immunoglobulin A Vasculitis (IgAV). It summarizes the results of clinical trials for various treatments including targeted-release budesonide, tonsillectomy, glucocorticoids, renin-angiotensin system inhibitors, and SGLT2 inhibitors.
This document provides a summary of the specific clinical questions and systematic review topics used in the development of the KDIGO 2025 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Immunoglobulin A Nephropathy (IgAN) and Immunoglobulin A Vasculitis (IgAV). It outlines the PICOD format used for these topics.
This document provides a summary of the specific evidence grading system used in the KDIGO 2025 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Immunoglobulin A Nephropathy (IgAN) and Immunoglobulin A Vasculitis (IgAV). It outlines the GRADE system and the criteria for grading the certainty of evidence.
This document provides a summary of the specific nomenclature and description used in the KDIGO 2025 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Immunoglobulin A Nephropathy (IgAN) and Immunoglobulin A Vasculitis (IgAV). It outlines the KDIGO nomenclature for grading recommendations and the determinants of the strength of recommendations.
This document provides a summary of the specific search strategies used in the systematic reviews conducted for the KDIGO 2025 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Immunoglobulin A Nephropathy (IgAN) and Immunoglobulin A Vasculitis (IgAV). It outlines the search strategies used to identify relevant studies.

Practice points are consensus-based statements representing the expert judgment of the Work Group and are not graded. Users should consider the practice point as expert guidance and use it as they see /uniFB01 t to inform the care of patients. Although these statements are developed based on a different methodology, they should not be seen as less important or a downgrade from graded recommendations.

The mission of Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) is to ' improve the care and outcomes of people with kidney disease worldwide through promoting coordination, collaboration, and integration of initiatives to develop and implement clinical practice guidelines. ' Since the publication in 2008 of the very /uniFB01 rst KDIGO guideline, devoted to hepatitis C and chronic kidney disease, KDIGO has published guidelines on many distinct topics. The Clinical Practice Guideline for Glomerular Diseases was published in 2021. The guideline chapters covering lupus nephritis and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody -associated vasculitis were updated in 2024. The 2025 updates of the Nephrotic Syndrome in Children and the IgA Nephropathy (IgAN) and IgAassociated Vasculitis (IgAV) guidelines re /uniFB02 ects the rapid growth of evidence in glomerular diseases in general and in nephrotic syndrome and IgAN in particular. While an updated evidence review was conducted for the chapter dedicated to minimal change disease, no new evidence was identi /uniFB01 ed; therefore, the 2021 chapter is still deemed current and valid.

Frequent updates to guidelines are important as new evidence is published. But frequent updates should not come at the expense of quality. KDIGO continuously strives to maintain the highest standards of excellence and to provide healthcare providers with the most relevant, evidence-based guidance, incorporating both recent advancements and widely accepted clinical standards through a systematic process. As such, the guideline updates include a combination of both graded recommendations and practice points as put forth in the KDIGO Methods Manual. Graded recommendations are based on a systematic review of the evidence and are graded for the strength of the recommendation (Level 1 or Level 2) and certainty of the evidence (A, ' high ' ; B, ' moderate ' ; C, ' low ' ; or D, ' very low ' ). Practice points are ungraded, consensus-based statements representing the expert judgment of the Work Group. Although practice points are issued when there has not been a systematic review, most practice points aim to inform the implementation of graded recommendations; they are often provided in a graphical format. Readers should consider practice points to be expert guidance or ' good practice statements ' and use them as they see /uniFB01 t to inform the care of patients.
